---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slidegreen

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size55[
Designing an Environmental Policy

+ Unequal Voice, Access and Impacts
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

Today we continue our work in the third section of our semester

- Our focus is on the real-world complications you should keep in mind as you work to design a policy.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

# Paper 3
.size40[
Propose a policy to address your selected environmental problem that balances the interests of the relevant stakeholders against the constraints of established institutions and uncertainty.

- Support all claims with evidence (e.g. academic literature, real-world examples, etc.)
]

???

I want to keep drawing your eyes back to the prompt for our final paper.

Remember, our work exploring complications is meant to:

1. help you keep this project moving forward in concrete ways, AND

2. deepen your thinking about environmental policy design.

<br>

### Questions on the prompt?

<br>

On Monday we discussed how collective action problems complicate environmental problem-solving.

- Let's make sure we're still clear on the two primary examples



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# Collective Action Problems (I)

<br>

## Rapid resource depletion when...

.size45[
- Resource use benefits the user, and

- The costs of use are shared by all users
]

???

The first type of collective action problem we discussed on Tuesday focused on the Hardin style Tragedy of the Commons dynamics.
- *Read formula on slide*

<br>

### Give me a real-world example of this collective action problem in action!

(SLIDE)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-ruined_pasture.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: bottom, inverse, textwhite

???

### How is a community grazing area an example of this dynamic in action?

<br>

### - How does the resource use benefit the user?
- (Free food for my cows!)

### - How are the costs of use shared by all users?
- (Everybody pays to feed my cows!)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-waste_river.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???


### How is pollution being dumped into the river an example of this dynamic in action?

<br>

### - How does the resource use benefit the user?
- (I need a place to put my pollution!)

### - How are the costs of use shared by all users?
- (Everybody who uses the river pays for my pollution!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# Collective Action Problems (I)

<br>

## Rapid resource depletion when...

.size45[
- Resource use benefits the user, and

- The costs of use are shared by all users
]

???

### Per this dynamic, under what conditions does this scenario produce the worst environmental outcomes?
- As the number of users of the resource increase, the costs of use by each shrinks and the likelihood of use increases
    - You "owned," e.g. expected access to, a vanishingly small share of the resource in the first place

- As the benefits of the resource increases for each user, the likelihood of use increases

<br>

### Any questions on this first collective action complication when trying to solve environmental problems?  




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# Collective Action Problems (II)

<br>

## Resource degradation when...

.size45[
- The costs of resource protection is borne by the user, and

- The benefits of resource protection are distributed widely
]

???

The second type of collective action problem we discussed on Tuesday was born from Mancur Olson's work on how groups function.
- *Read formula on slide*

<br>

### What label does the literature call the users under this dynamic?
- (Free riders!)

### What is a free-rider?
- Someone who enjoys the benefits of other peoples' efforts without contributing to the provision of those benefits

<br>

### Give me some real world examples to illustrate this dynamic
- (SLIDE)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-2-IPCC_Sources_Emissions.png')
background-size: 65%
background-position: center
class: middle, slidegreen

???

### How is climate change a good example of the free-rider problem?

- The costs of resource protection is borne by the user
    - Basically EVERY action you take creates emissions or contributes to the creation of emissions
    
- The benefits of resource protection are distributed widely
    - Averting climate change is a benefit to the world, BUT...
    - Your personal contribution to climate change is VANISHINGLY small. 
    - If you stopped all your activities that made climate change worse it would cost you a ton and the climate system would not notice.

<br>

### Notes
- https://ar5-syr.ipcc.ch/topic_observedchanges.php
- IPCC. Climate Change 2014 Synthesis Report, Fifth Assessment Report.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# Collective Action Problems (II)

<br>

## Resource degradation when...

.size45[
- The costs of resource protection is borne by the user, and

- The benefits of resource protection are distributed widely
]

???

### Per this dynamic, under what conditions does this scenario produce the worst environmental outcomes?
- Free-riding incentives correspond to:
    - The size of the group of users
    - The complexity of the problem
    - The size of the impact of your use on the system

<br>

### Any questions on this second collective action complication when trying to solve environmental problems?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-climate_change_is_racism.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: top, center, inverse

???

Today I'd like us to explore how the role of inequality and racism complicates our efforts to solve environmental problems.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-climate_change_is_racism.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: top, center, inverse

.size40[.textwhite[**How do the problematic roots of American environmentalism impact the processes of problem-solving and outcomes we observe today?**]]

???

Specifically, I want us to think about these challenge as policy designers.
- Pretty clearly the research shows us that the roots of conservatism are problematic, and

- We know inequality plays a pervasive role in the functioning of our political system.

<br>

Our job is to note these as complications and always be on the lookout for opportunities to adapt our policy designs to either address them or, at least, to not make them worse.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-tragedy_of_the_tragedy.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Let's start with this piece by Mildenberger (2019).

We can separate this argument into two pieces:
1. Hardin used ecological causes to advance some super problematic policies, and
2. This has made addressing climate change WAY harder for us today.

<br>

Let's begin with this first argument.

### According to Mildenberger (2019), why is Hardin an incredibly problematic figure?

- Hugely cited (40k!) and often assigned
- Southern Poverty Law Center labels him a white nationalist
- His writings and political activism helped inspire the anti-immigrant hatred spilling across America today
- Advocated “lifeboat ethics” (e.g. lobbied Congress against sending food aid to poor nations, because he believed their populations were threatening Earth’s “carrying capacity.”)
- He got the history of the commons wrong



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12-2-Hardin_Google_Scholar.png')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center
class: bottom, center, slidegreen

#### Source: Google Scholar (2022-04-03)

???

There's no getting around this key element: the Tragedy of the Commons is probably the most cited piece of environmental policy research in modern history.

<br>

Unfortunately, when professors and other researchers in the social sciences seek out work on environmental problems they inevitably start with this.

<br>

So, we need to spend a few minutes evaluating why this is such bad news for us in the real world.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-Hardin.webp')
background-size: 35%
background-position: right
class: bottom, slidered

.left[

.size65[**Garrett Hardin**]

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

<br>

.size35[.content-box-red[1) Human reproduction is a "Tragedy of the Commons"]]

.size35[.content-box-red[2) Freedom to breed brings ruin to us all]]

.size35[.content-box-red[3) History proves me right!]]
]

???

Say hello to Garrett Hardin.

<br>

Hardin's work is meant to build a broader argument using these three premises.

1. Human reproduction operates as a global Tragedy of the Commons 
    - Having kids benefits the parent
    - The costs of keeping those kids alive are paid by the world
    - THEREFORE, all parents should choose to have more kids than they otherwise could support on their own

2. The freedom to breed brings ruin to us all
    - Without restrictions, humans will breed the planet to destruction

3. History proves me right!

<br>

SLIDE: And the big conclusion of this argument?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-lifeboat.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: top, right, inverse

.textwhite[.size60[**Lifeboat Ethics**]]

???

We must embrace the ethics of a lifeboat!

<br>

Hardin asks us to imagine that:
- each "rich" country in the world is a lifeboat,

- each poor country is an over-flowing lifeboat, and

- emigration is a continuous process where "the poor fall out of their lifeboats and swim for a while in the water outside, hoping to be admitted to a rich lifeboat or in some other way to benefit from the 'goodies' on our rich lifeboats

- If a lifeboat chooses to admit these poor swimmers they will swamp your boat and you'll all die.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-lifeboat.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: top, right, inverse

.textwhite[.size60[**Lifeboat Ethics**]]

???

In policy terms, Hardin argues the rich world should:

- Control the right to reproduction to prevent us from filling up our lifeboat,

- seal our borders to protect our lifeboat,

- Let poor countries wipe themselves out if they make bad choices, and

- Ignore suffering in the developing world as punishment for accepting bad governments that do not appropriately prepare for disasters.

### Pretty dark, huh?

<br>

Key to this outrageous set of policy prescriptions are the premises of his argument.

The questions for us are:

- Is the tragedy of the commons inevitable? AND

- Is human reproduction an example of this dynamic in action?

<br>

SLIDE: Let's evaluate the tragedy and the idea that letting people choose to reproduce is likely to lead to destruction.





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-interrogation.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: bottom

???

According to Hardin a tragedy is inevitable and unavoidable because of the nature of the commons.

- In short, Hardin asks us to think of the users of a shared commons as players in a one-shot Prisoner's Dilemma.

- Each player is compelled to defect because that is our dominant strategy.

--

.center[.size35[.content-box-blue[The essence of dramatic tragedy is not unhappiness.]

.content-box-blue[It resides in the solemnity of the remorseless]

.content-box-blue[working of things.]]]

???

<br>

Hardin defends this logic using this famous Whitehead quote (p1244).

In his writing Hardin refers to our destruction like the working of an engine.

- It does what it must based on its design without thought or compunction (e.g. guilt).

- The engine doesn't choose to destroy itself, it simply functions as it was designed.

<br>

Going further, Hardin is arguing that this logic explains why unconstrained human reproduction will end in tragedy for our planet.

- Each parent is compelled to defect because that is our dominant strategy.



---

class: middle, center

.size50[.center[.content-box-blue[**The Prisoner's Dilemma**]]]

<br>

<br>

```{css, echo=F}
/* Change the background color to white for shaded rows (even rows) */
.remark-slide thead, .remark-slide tr:nth-child(2n) {
      background-color: white;
}
```

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("You", ""),
  col2 = c("No Kids", "Have Kids"),
  Cooperate = c("No pay, no gain", "They pay, you gain"),
  Defect = c("You pay, no gain", "You pay, you gain")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("l", "l", "c", "c"), col.names = c("", "", "No Kids", "Have Kids")) |>
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "Other Parents" = 2)) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1, bold = TRUE, width = "8em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 2, bold = TRUE, width = "15em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 3, width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 4, width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 35, bootstrap_options = "basic")
```

???

*Step through the logic in the table*

### Why is having kids the dominant strategy?

<br>

### Given our discussion of the prisoner's dilemma, what are the key assumptions necessary to argue that human reproduction is a tragedy of the commons that will end in our destruction?

1. Assumes your decision to have kids is influenced by a fear that other people having kids impacts you
    - Don't have kids means you pay just for theirs
    - Have kids and they pay for yours
    - *Not sure I've accurately translated Hardin's argument here*

2. Assumes parents have INSANELY short time horizons and MASSIVE discount rates
    - This assumes parents completely ignore the costs imposed on future generations, AND
    - That they ignore the costs imposed on their own children, AND
    - That they ignore the costs they themselves and their children will have to pay right now

3. Assumes we only get to play the game ONE time with no ability to talk to the other users

4. Assumes we have no ability to design institutions that can help us manage the problems we face.




---

class: middle, center

.size50[.center[.content-box-blue[**The Prisoner's Dilemma**]]]

<br>

<br>

```{css, echo=F}
/* Change the background color to white for shaded rows (even rows) */
.remark-slide thead, .remark-slide tr:nth-child(2n) {
      background-color: white;
}
```

```{r}
tibble(
  col1 = c("You", ""),
  col2 = c("No Kids", "Have Kids"),
  Cooperate = c("No pay, no gain", "They pay, you gain"),
  Defect = c("You pay, no gain", "You pay, you gain")
) |>
  kableExtra::kbl(align = c("l", "l", "c", "c"), col.names = c("", "", "No Kids", "Have Kids")) |>
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "Other Parents" = 2)) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 1, bold = TRUE, width = "8em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 2, bold = TRUE, width = "15em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 3, width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(column = 4, width = "25em") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 35, bootstrap_options = "basic")
```

???

### These are borderline insane assumptions about parents, so why make them?

- Because you're trying to justify a racism!
    - e.g. lifeboat ethics
    
<br>

SLIDE: **Anybody want to guess the reaction from historians to these claims?**



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-Truman_show_what_the_hell.gif')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

According to historians, Hardin completely misrepresents the history of commons resources he claimed to be drawing from.

<br>

It seems Hardin has a series of aggressive, white nationalist policy conclusions he wanted to support and he decided environmentalism would allow him to get where he wanted to go.

- Motivated reasoning is one hell of a drug!

<br>

Let's talk two quick examples of what history ACTUALLY teaches us about the commons and societal collapse.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-pasture_farming.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

As Dr. Susan Cox thoroughly explains in her article for *Environmental Ethics* the medieval and post-medieval commons lands of England did not experience Hardin's tragedy.

**The bottom line was the English commons system "succeeded admirably in its time."**

1. The English "commons" severely restricted access
    + "...the English common was not available to the general public but rather only to certain individuals who inherited or were granted the right to use it, and use of the common even by these people was not unregulated."

2. The English "commons" were heavily regulated
    + "The types and in some cases the numbers of animals each tenant could pasture were limited, based at least partly on a recognition of the limited carrying capacity of the land."
    
3. The end of the "commons" was politics, not dramatic tragedy
    + "The decline of the commons system" came due to "widespread abuse of the rules governing the commons, land “reforms” chiefly designed to increase the holdings of a few landowners, improved agricultural techniques, and the effects of the industrial revolution."

<br>

So, if the ToC wasn't evident in the historical commons, what about other societies that collapsed after wasting their resources?

Was Hardin's explanation right for them?
- Hint: No, it is not.

<br>

## Notes
+ *Image: British Library - Milking sheep in the pen and maids carrying the milk, manuscript illumination, anonymous in the Luttrell Psalter, c. 1340*

+ Cox, Susan. (1985). No Tragedy on the Commons. *Environmental Ethics*. Vol 7, Issue 1, p49-61.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-Anasazi_Mystery.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: inverse

.textwhite[.size70[**The Chaco Anasazi**]]

???

### Anybody ever heard of the Chaco Anasazi or visited the cliffside pueblos they built?

The Chaco Anasazi, meaning the Ancient Ones, had a sizable civilization for some 500 years in the southwest US (from about 600 AD to 1100 AD)

+ San Juan Basin area of New Mexico

They were a flourishing and dynamic society.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-Anasazi_Trade_Map.jpg')
background-size: 50%
background-position: left
class: inverse

.right[.textwhite[.size70[**The Chaco Anasazi**]]]

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='50%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/13-1-turquoise_beads.webp')
```

???

Archaelogical evidence has shown us they maintained extensive trade ties across very large distances.

+ Evidence of turquoise beads from across the western US [Link](https://www.livescience.com/44703-turquoise-trade-network-revealed.html)

+ This trade network spanned several states: Colorado, Nevada and southeastern California

<br>

Some impressive networks built and supported over centuries.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-anasazi3.jpg')
background-size: 50%
background-position: left
class: inverse

.right[.textwhite[.size70[**The Chaco Anasazi**]]]

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='45%'}
knitr::include_graphics('libs/Images/13-1-Anasazi-pot.jpeg')
```

???

The Anasazi also built extensive public works to allow for city building and irrigation for larger farm lands

+ Damming rivers, managing forests, roads, etc

Also supported a thriving arts and science culture.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-anasazi2.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: inverse, top

.center[.textwhite[.size55[**Why did the Anasazi disappear?**]]]

???

*Source: Jared Diamond's Collapse book has such stories (although historians quibble with MANY of his details!)*

The question is, for a society as incredibly successful as they were, how did they disappear so rapidly?

<br>

They lived in a place, US Southwest, whose environment was already somewhat strained (low rainfall, not a lot of forest land)
- As their population grew, demands on environment grew and environmental resources declined

- They tried a ton of policies to grow: Damming rivers, extensive trade, going further and further to find trees

- The problem was a long drought hit them and they were screwed.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/13-1-anasazi2.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: inverse, top

.center[.textwhite[.size55[**Why did the Anasazi disappear?**]]]

???

The lessons of their collapse?
- Never just one thing that wipes out a society,

- The idea that humans are compelled to race to the end because it is the end is not evident in the historical record.

- May not be able to anticipate when conditions will change

<br>

Contra Hardin, the lesson is **NOT** that humans are compelled to double-down on bad strategies and selfish abuses of the land.

The actual story of societal collapse tied to environmental mismanagement is always a much more complicated one.

- Dare I say it, a political one?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/07-1-tragedy_of_the_tragedy.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

### Now, what is Mildenberger's (2019) argument about how Hardin has made the climate change problem so much harder to address?

<br>

### Are we convinced by this? Why or why not?

<br>

### As policy designers, are we persuaded by Mildenberger's suggested reframing of climate change?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-kashwan.png')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Kashwan, P. (2020, September 2). American environmentalism’s racist roots have shaped global thinking about conservation. *The Conversation*.

### What is the key thesis in Kashwan's (2020) argument?
- (SLIDE)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-kashwan.png')
background-size: 45%
background-position: top
class: bottom, center

.size30[
"American environmentalism’s racist roots have influenced global conservation practices. Most notably, they are embedded in longstanding prejudices against local communities and a focus on protecting pristine wildernesses. This dominant narrative pays little thought to indigenous and other poor people who rely on these lands – even when they are its most effective stewards" (Kashwan 2020).
]

???

### Were you convinced by this argument? Why or why not?

<br>

### How do the problematic roots of American environmentalism impact the processes of problem-solving and outcomes we observe today?

### - What is the "better model" Kashwan argues we should embrace?

- "Socially just nature conservation is possible under two main conditions: Indigenous and rural communities have concrete stakes in protecting those resources and can participate in policy decisions" (Kashwan 2020).




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[
Bullard, R. D., Mohai, P., Saha, R., & Wright, B. (2007). Toxic Wastes and Race at Twenty: Why Race Still Matters After All of These Years. United Church of Christ Justice & Witness Ministries. (**ONLY ch 3 and 4**)
]

???

### What do we learn from Bullard et al ch 3 and ch 4?

<br>

### How should we address this in our environmental policy problem-solving?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/08-1-env_justice.jpg')
background-size: 86%
background-position: center
class: top

???

### Ok, where do we end up at this point?

#### - Are we convinced that the american environmental movement is built on and perpetuates racist outcomes?

*DISCUSS*
